author: @johnstone-gh9nj
text:
Hello Robert,
   Thanks for all your efforts.  I'm just wondering In all the different vawt designs you have made.  What one would you recommend to run dc power to preheat a 15/20 gal domestic water cyclinder.  I'm In a wide open area.

author: @solarcabin
text:
The problem with any VAWT is that as the wind pushes against one side of the blades to turn the generator the other side has to push against the wind losing energy and slowing the VAWT.

To overcome that you can create a shroud that directs the wind to only one side of the blades so there is no loss from the other side.

I demonstrate this in my Eagle VAWT Turbine design on my channel.

author: @RyanJBarnard
text: Have you thought about stacked turbines with every other turbine rotation in the opposite direction? I was thinking that you could have the magnets on the top of one turbine and the wire coil on the bottom of the stacked turbine. This would double the speed of rotation without the need for a gearbox. By making a system of couner-rotating stackable generators you could generate greater energy than a commercial wind turbine while keeping the parts small enough to print off on a 3D printer.

author: @resoundingecho
text: How does the difference in fluid collision affect it? The Pelton wheel receives a focused jet of water with a fixed direction and cross sectional area (smaller than the cup size) that splits on the center ridge and transfers its energy to the wheel before being ejected out the sides. The Savonius wheel receives an unfocused wall of air which is not split, though excess air pours over the edges of the cup/trough. The best way I can visualize the Savonius is as a trough filled to the brim with water where the angle perpendicular to the water is the direction of air flow and the surface area of the water is the effective area being acted on by the air (the more it it tilted the less water you contain and the smaller the surface area). Considering this difference, I can also see why the added curved plate would improve the Savonius since it increases the surface area and directs some of the wind that would otherwise flow past into the cup. You may be able to improve the C-rotor or Lens rotor by changing the plate with airfoils (maybe adding vortex generators) that minimize air separation, directing more air into the cup.

author: @rudolfvanacker8569
text: It would be a good project for Luke to determine the airflow through the rotor. Use a length of light wool thread attached to a fine needle in front of the rotor in a steady air flow with the rotor fixed. Move the needle across the whole rotor and check the airflow. Index the rotor in 5 or 10 deg increments through 120 deg, repeating the process. May indicate any improvements required to the profiles of the blades.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: I found that the best, most accurate way to test and compare wind turbine designs is to have a standard for airflow like a wind tunnel. If he is serious about this that's what he should do.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: same size doesn't matter much mate - just normalize for size - that is make torque a proportion of rotor diameter, rotor height and wind speed and you can compare easily enough

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringKeep it simple!!!    Just a no-load speed test, of the same size VAWT rotors, on the same stand and bearings, and within a half hour in the same outdoor wind, will give very useful information.  When a new competition size VAWT is made a quick test against the top speed rotor will give a idea of its usefulness as a power generator. It would be very hard to come up with a "normalizing formula" that everyone would agree on.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
To be brutally honest, to say something is 'better' requires some really extensive comparisons, simultaneously, at scale, and that will likely take quite some bit of time, so for those seeking an instant result...

That's not going to happen.

Were I wanting to produce a comparison, I suspect it would take me no less than 6 months of printing parts, and another 6 months of putting the test specimens through their paces under identical conditions that varied throughout a decent slice of a year.

In the mean time, I figure I shall just sit back and enjoy the sharing of novel ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ain't that the truth lol

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Maybe with time somebody can set up some simple test stands and put them somewhere that do not need intensive maintenance to test this.

Some little stations put in places people might deem 'different' enough that the test stands each kinda-sorta represent differing conditions [from 'optimal' to 'worst-case'] and then come back to them, say, once a month, to pick up data they have recorded, maybe on a SD card or other cheap memory devices and log it all into some kind of program that can fairly and equally compare all of the information.

It would take putting copies of each device at each station, which is a bid of work, and expense, so this might be something people would want to donate to, in the name of science...

At each chosen location, place one copy of every variant that was being tested, at what is deemed a proper functional distance from the other test subjects [in other words, not crowd them all together one one sheet of builder's board, because people will say that one is interfering with the optimal performance of its neighbor and therefore rendering the results invalid] say on little posts driven into the ground [might need government permits, depending on where the tests are being conducted, but as a nonprofit scientific research, especially if you had some academia willing to help give it credibility, and it being a temporary, non-invasive, environmentally respectful manner, should not be too hard to get] and just let the little test outposts all do what they can do and record it all and then post the results.

Could even post it on your favorite site Google Scholar.
